title: Data Preparation & Preprocessing
section: BERT vs Gen AI: Sentiment Analysis of Women’s E-commerce Clothing Reviews
text:
BERT vs Gen AI: Sentiment Analysis of Women’s E-commerce Clothing Reviews

MSc Business Analytics · NLP & Sentiment Analysis · Ariane Damian · Apr. 17, 2026

title: Data Preparation & Preprocessing
text:
Introduction
Consumer reviews have long served as a cornerstone of business reputation and trust. In the digital age, the volume and velocity of online feedback has made manual analysis increasingly unsustainable, giving rise to the need for automated sentiment analysis.
This report compares two NLP models for binary sentiment classification of women’s e-commerce clothing reviews:

Evaluate BERT (encoder-based, discriminative) as a fine-tuned classification model
Evaluate LLaMA3 (decoder-based, generative) using zero-shot prompting
Assess both models across key performance metrics: accuracy, F1-score, precision, and recall
Discuss business relevance, ethical considerations, and limitations for e-commerce deployment

title: Data Preparation & Preprocessing
text:
Methodology

Model Selection
Two transformer-based NLP models were selected to reflect diferent approaches to Natural Language Understanding tasks:








Model
Type
Approach




BERT
Encoder-based (discriminative)
Fine-tuned on labelled dataset via supervised learning


LLaMA3
Decoder-based (generative)
Zero-shot prompting — no training or labelled examples required





Dataset

Source: Kaggle — real-world women’s e-commerce clothing reviews
~23,500 records and 10 attributes prior to cleaning
Target variable: Recommended IND (binary: 1 = Recommended, 0 = Not Recommended)
Sentiment distribution: 82% Recommended / 18% Not Recommended (imbalanced)



Visualisation of Class Imbalance - Through Python


Code
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

# ── Counts taken directly from official dataset──────────────────────────
counts = [4101, 18540]          # [Not Recommended (0), Recommended (1)]
bar_labels   = ['Not Recommended (0)', 'Recommended (1)']
bar_colors   = ['#E8735A', '#6DBF8A']

# ── Plot ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 5))

# Left – Count bar chart
axes[0].bar(
    bar_labels,
    counts,
    color=bar_colors,
    edgecolor='black'
)
axes[0].set_title('Class Distribution', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Number of Reviews')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Sentiment Label')
for i, v in enumerate(counts):
    axes[0].text(i, v + 100, f'{v:,}',
                 ha='center', fontweight='bold')

# Right – Percentage pie chart
axes[1].pie(
    counts,
    labels=['Not Recommended (0)', 'Recommended (1)'],
    colors=bar_colors,
    autopct='%1.1f%%',
    startangle=90,
    explode=(0.05, 0.05)
)
axes[1].set_title('Class Distribution (%)', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()










Null values in Review Text (845 total) were removed as this column functions as the model input
A stratified sample of 2,000 records was extracted to preserve class distribution
Train/test split: 1,600 training / 400 test reviews (80/20, random seed 42)
Class weights were applied to penalise misclassifications in the minority class (Not Recommended)
BERT tokenisation used WordPiece Tokenizer (30,522 tokens); truncation threshold set at 128 tokens based on median review length analysis



Model Training & Prompting

BERT was fine-tuned using an AdamW optimiser (corrects weight decay issues present in standard Adam) at a learning rate of 2e-5 and batch size of 16, trained over 3 epochs
LLaMA3 was evaluated locally via zero-shot prompting using a structured prompt with three elements:








Prompt Element
Content




Context
You are classifying women’s clothing reviews


Instruction
Classify the review as either RECOMMENDED or NOT RECOMMENDED


Constraint
Only reply with one of those two labels, nothing else





Performance Metrics
Accuracy, F1-score, precision, and recall were all assessed. Due to the class imbalance, accuracy alone is insufficient — it risks biasing the model toward the majority class, violating the EU’s Trustworthy AI requirement of Fairness. F1-score was prioritised as the balance between precision and recall.

title: Data Preparation & Preprocessing
text:
Summary of Findings

BERT Fine-Tuning
Training loss declined consistently across all 3 epochs, demonstrating genuine learning rather than memorisation. Key loss comparisons:




Starting Loss
Ending Loss
Loss Reduction




1 Epoch
0.6186
0.3705
0.2461


3 Epochs
0.5604
0.0174
0.5430




Training on 3 epochs yielded a loss reduction nearly twice that of 1 epoch
Some volatility remained at the 3rd epoch, suggesting scope for further refinement



Training Loss by Epoch - Python Recreated Plot


Code
#code-fold: show
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

np.random.seed(42)

# ── Helper: simulate batch loss with smooth rolling average ──────────────────
def make_losses(n_batches, start, end, noise=0.18):
    trend = np.linspace(start, end, n_batches)
    noise_arr = np.random.normal(0, noise, n_batches)
    batch_loss = np.clip(trend + noise_arr, 0.05, 1.2)
    rolling = np.convolve(batch_loss, np.ones(10)/10, mode='valid')
    return batch_loss, rolling

# ── Fig 3.11a — 1 Epoch (100 batches) ───────────────────────────────────────
batch_loss_1, rolling_1 = make_losses(100, start=0.6186, end=0.3705)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))
ax.plot(batch_loss_1, color='#7ec8e3', linewidth=0.9, label='Batch loss')
ax.plot(range(9, 100), rolling_1, color='red', linewidth=2, label='10-batch rolling avg')
ax.set_title('Training Loss per Batch — 1 Epoch')
ax.set_xlabel('Batch number')
ax.set_ylabel('Loss')
ax.legend(fontsize=9)
ax.text(0.01, 0.05,
        f'Starting loss:  0.6186\nEnding loss:    0.3705\nLoss reduction: 0.2481',
        transform=ax.transAxes, fontsize=8, verticalalignment='bottom',
        fontfamily='monospace')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('fig_3_11a.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
print("Note: Batch loss values are simulated to reflect reported statistics.")

# ── Fig 3.11b — 3 Epochs (300 batches) ──────────────────────────────────────
# Each epoch: loss resets slightly higher then trends down again
b1, r1 = make_losses(100, start=0.5604, end=0.25)
b2, r2 = make_losses(100, start=0.30,   end=0.12)
b3, r3 = make_losses(100, start=0.15,   end=0.0174, noise=0.08)

batch_loss_3 = np.concatenate([b1, b2, b3])
rolling_3    = np.convolve(batch_loss_3, np.ones(10)/10, mode='valid')

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4))
ax.plot(batch_loss_3, color='#7ec8e3', linewidth=0.9, label='Batch loss')
ax.plot(range(9, 300), rolling_3, color='red', linewidth=2, label='10-batch rolling avg')

# Epoch boundary lines
ax.axvline(x=100, color='red',   linestyle='--', linewidth=1.2, label='Epoch 1 end')
ax.axvline(x=200, color='green', linestyle='--', linewidth=1.2, label='Epoch 2 end')

ax.set_title('Training Loss per Batch — 3 Epochs')
ax.set_xlabel('Batch number')
ax.set_ylabel('Loss')
ax.legend(fontsize=9)
ax.text(0.01, 0.05,
        f'Starting loss (batch 1):   0.5604\nEnding loss (batch 300): 0.0174\nLoss reduction:              0.5430',
        transform=ax.transAxes, fontsize=8, verticalalignment='bottom',
        fontfamily='monospace')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('fig_3_11b.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
print("Note: Batch loss values are simulated to reflect reported statistics.")









Note: Batch loss values are simulated to reflect reported statistics.









Note: Batch loss values are simulated to reflect reported statistics.




BERT Evaluation Results
BERT performed strongly for the majority class (Recommended) and respectably for the minority class (Not Recommended):










Metric
Recommended (1)
Not Recommended (0)
Macro Avg
Weighted Avg




F1-Score
0.95
0.76
0.85
0.92


Precision
0.94
0.80
0.87
0.92


Recall
0.96
0.72
0.84
0.92




Precision outperforms recall for the minority class (0.80 vs 0.72) — the model accurately flags negatives but misses 28% of actual negatives
Overall accuracy: 91.75%



LLaMA3 Zero-Shot Evaluation Results
LLaMA3 demonstrated high recall for Not Recommended (0.97) but very low precision (0.57) — indicating it over-identifies negative reviews, classifying 43% as false positives:










Metric
Recommended (1)
Not Recommended (0)
Macro Avg
Weighted Avg




F1-Score
0.91
0.72
0.81
0.87


Precision
0.99
0.57
0.78
0.92


Recall
0.83
0.97
0.90
0.86




Overall accuracy: 86.00%
High recall bias toward Not Recommended likely a consequence of the absence of fine-tuning



Head-to-Head Comparison



Metric
BERT
LLaMA3




Overall Accuracy
91.75%
86.00%


Macro F1
0.85
0.81


Precision — Not Recommended
0.80
0.57


Recall — Not Recommended
0.72
0.97


Training Required
Yes (30 min)
No




BERT outperforms LLaMA3 across accuracy, Macro F1, and precision for negative sentiment
LLaMA3 achieves competitive results without any training or labelled data

title: Data Preparation & Preprocessing
text:
Discussion

Business Relevance

For e-commerce organisations, LLaMA3 is the more accessible option — requiring no data labelling, training infrastructure, or deep technical knowledge
However, BERT is substantially more reliable at detecting genuine negative reviews, which carries real operational risk if misclassified (e.g. misdirected resources, unnecessary escalations)
A practical compromise is DistilBERT — a derivative of BERT operating at 97% of its capacity, 60% faster and 40% smaller — prioritising rapid deployment over exhaustive complexity



Ethics & Responsible AI

The dataset is anonymised and from an authentic source — consistent with EU Trustworthy AI requirements under Privacy and Data Governance
Class imbalance introduces bias risk, particularly violating the Fairness principle if the minority class is under-represented in training
LLaMA3’s zero-shot bias toward negative classification raises additional concerns around reliability in production environments

title: Data Preparation & Preprocessing
text:
Recommendations
The following directions are recommended for future work and deployment considerations:

Evaluate DistilBERT as a production-ready alternative to BERT — nearly equivalent performance at significantly lower resource cost
Explore few-shot prompting with LLaMA3 to assess whether providing examples improves precision for the minority class
Conduct hyperparameter tuning on BERT (learning rate, batch size, sample size) to address remaining volatility at epoch 3
Establish a control group (no class weights) to formally measure the impact of class weighting on model performance
Extend to multi-class sentiment classification to explore whether the metric uniformity observed in weighted averages persists across more than two labels







Note



BERT demonstrated stronger and more balanced performance for the binary classification objective, with higher accuracy and more reliable detection of negative sentiment. LLaMA3’s zero-shot results, while slightly weaker, highlight the growing potential of generative models for classification tasks — particularly in resource-constrained or low-expertise environments.

My self-confidence regarding coding languages, particularly HTML/CSS and JavaScript, has grown tremendously throughout this process, and I'm grateful for the opportunity to showcase my work.

I'm especially proud of the pop-up windows and quirky sounds, and I hope that whoever comes across this site gets the same level of enjoyment I did when interacting with all the elements :D

title: Ariane Damian
section: Fictitious Finances: Facts and Fallacies on Fraud
text:
Fictitious Finances: Facts and Fallacies on Fraud

MSc Business Analytics · Fraud Analysis · Ariane Damian · Dec. 03, 2025

title: Ariane Damian
text:
Introduction
Fraud has existed since ancient times, with detection and prevention methods evolving in parallel. The digital age has dramatically accelerated both the sophistication of fraud and the tools used to combat it.
This project analyses a financial dataset to:

Identify demographics most susceptible to committing financial crimes
Flag key parameters indicating suspicious or fraudulent behaviour
Perform correlation and outlier analysis
Lay the groundwork for a future predictive fraud detection model

title: Ariane Damian
text:
Methodology

Data Preparation

No missing values were identified in the dataset
Numeric currency columns were formatted as Currency; other numeric columns retained as Whole Number or Decimal
Binary (0/1) columns were converted to Yes/No for Power BI visualisation
Variables such as Age, Credit Score, and Account Age were grouped into categories for more effective analysis



Key Statistical Analysis
The following statistical methods were applied:







Method
Purpose




Mean, Median, Standard Deviation
Summarise central tendency and spread


Correlation Analysis
Determine existence and strength of variable relationships


Interquartile Range (IQR)
Identify outliers and understand data distribution

title: Ariane Damian
text:
Summary of Findings

Basic Demographic Overview
Fraud rate and median transaction amounts were examined across key demographic groups.







Demographic
Key Observation




Age 70+
Fraud cases exceed non-fraud cases — mirrors real-world research on older adults’ vulnerability


Age 90+
Highest median fraudulent transaction amount, but likely an outlier effect (<1% of total data)


Credit Score
Lower (worst-performing) credit score groups show higher fraud frequency


Education — PhD
Lowest fraud rate but highest fraud value — fraud is rare but high-value


Gender / Device Type
Minimal differences observed across groups





Other relevant findings include

Transaction thresholds (upper limit ~€6,000) and outlier patterns provide useful signals for fraud detection
Correlation analysis revealed mostly weak relationships, with the most actionable being income–fraud and loan amount–fraud connections
While IQR is effective for identifying unusual figures in some variables, it is not universally applicable. Fraud can and does occur within “normal” data ranges.

title: Ariane Damian
text:
Recommendations
The following improvements are recommended for future analysis and model development:

Incorporate regression coefficients and confidence intervals for more rigorous statistical analysis
Perform complete outlier removal to improve model accuracy
Expand outlier analysis beyond IQR to include Z-scores and multivariate methods
Apply findings to machine learning algorithms to build a robust, predictive fraud detection model
Consider additional variables not fully explored in this report to reduce noise and improve signal detection







Note



All analytical factors identified in this project — demographic patterns, correlation signals, and outlier thresholds — can serve as inputs for machine learning models capable of more sophisticated and automated fraud detection.

title: Ariane Damian
section: An Examination into Star Ratings Based on the Official Yelp Dataset
text:
An Examination into Star Ratings Based on the Official Yelp Dataset

MSc Business Analytics · Business Analytics Project · Ariane Damian · Dec. 02, 2025

title: Ariane Damian
text:
Introduction
Reputation is a direct reflection of a business’ performance, influencing consumer behaviour through perceived image, reliability, and quality. With the proliferation of technology, online review platforms have become essential for determining business reputation — and Yelp is one of the most established.
This project analyses the official Yelp dataset to:

Understand the distribution of businesses, star ratings, and reviews across the platform
Identify key attributes and characteristics that influence star ratings
Apply and evaluate regression models to predict star ratings
Propose a new analytics-based business model for Yelp centred on Star Rating Prediction

title: Ariane Damian
text:
Methodology

Data Preparation

Raw dataset consisted of approximately 150,000 rows and 14 columns
Missing and null values were removed
Columns hours, address, and postal_code were excluded as irrelevant to the target variable
Categories and Attributes were split by frequency into 12 key categories and 33 key attributes
Final cleaned dataset: 150,243 rows and 56 columns



Modelling Techniques
Three regression models were applied to predict Star Rating:







Model
Purpose




Linear Regression (LR)
Baseline model; explores possible linear relationships between variables and star rating


Random Forest Regression (RFR)
Handles non-linear relationships; robust to outliers and missing data


Gradient Boosting Regression (GBR)
Advanced model; iteratively corrects errors to maximise predictive accuracy

title: Ariane Damian
text:
Summary of Findings

Exploratory Data Analysis
Patterns in business distribution, star ratings, and attributes were examined across the dataset.







Finding
Key Observation




Business Categories
Food & Restaurants dominates with ~65,000 businesses — a heavily saturated market


Star Ratings
Most businesses rated between 3–4.5 stars; 4 stars is the most common


Category vs. Rating
Most categories cluster near the 3.6 average — the individual business, not its category, drives rating


Review Count
Most businesses receive very few reviews; review count does not significantly correlate with star rating


Business Attributes
Most attributes (e.g. outdoor seating, delivery, bike parking), when present, are associated with higher ratings





Other relevant findings include

The market for Pets and Hotels/Travel categories is less saturated, offering greater opportunity for smaller businesses to build customer relationships
Low review counts may indicate low visibility or neutral satisfaction — both addressable through Yelp’s advertising and analytics services
Growing the review sample size by increasing customer engagement would also improve the accuracy of the Star Rating Prediction model over time




Regression Analysis
All three models were evaluated using R², Mean Absolute Error (MAE), and Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE).







Model
Performance Summary




Linear Regression
Weakest performer; lowest R², highest MAE & RMSE; ~1 star average deviation


Random Forest
Slightly better overall; still approximately 1 star deviation across both datasets


Gradient Boosting
Marginal improvements in some metrics; not decisively better than RFR




Random Forest Regression was identified as the most robust model, though differences between models were marginal







Important



All models are limited to business-level data; incorporating review text sentiment and pricing information would likely improve predictions substantially.

title: Ariane Damian
text:
Recommendations
The following improvements and opportunities are recommended for Yelp and future model development:

Develop a premium Star Rating Prediction service that businesses can subscribe to for performance forecasting and improvement guidance
Use Star Rating predictions internally to refine Yelp’s ranking algorithms and “Best of Yelp” promotional lists
Incorporate review text sentiment analysis and price-level data to significantly improve model accuracy
Expand modelling to address class imbalance in ratings (few low-rated businesses) and category imbalance (dominance of food businesses)
Offer targeted advertising services to businesses with low review counts to increase visibility and grow the dataset
